items.q: What distinguishes structural isomers from stereoisomers?
items.correct: Structural isomers have different atom connectivity; stereoisomers have the same connectivity but different spatial arrangement.
items.misconception: Stereoisomers are just structural isomers drawn from a different angle.
items.random_wrong: Stereoisomers have different numbers of atoms but the same molecular formula.
items.why: Students don't always grasp that 3D arrangement is chemically meaningful; rotating a drawing on paper feels like the same molecule, so stereoisomerism feels artificial.

items.q: How is a dynamic equilibrium different from a static one?
items.correct: In a dynamic equilibrium, forward and reverse reactions continue at equal rates; in a static equilibrium, nothing is happening at the particle level.
items.misconception: Dynamic and static equilibria are the same thing because both show constant macroscopic properties.
items.random_wrong: A dynamic equilibrium means the system is still changing visibly.
items.why: Macroscopically both look identical (no observable change), so students don't grasp the crucial particle-level distinction.

items.q: What are the units of Kc for a reaction?
items.correct: They depend on Δn (the difference between moles of gaseous/aqueous products and reactants) — Kc can be dimensionless or have units like mol dm^-3.
items.misconception: Kc always has units of mol dm^-3.
items.why: Students learn Kc with a fixed unit example and don't realise units arise from the exponents in the expression; for A + B ⇌ C + D, Kc is unitless, but for A ⇌ B + C, Kc has units.
It's mixture. “element” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Best method to separate ink colours:
items.why: Correct answer: chromatography. “distillation” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
Exact answer: 3. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
